key_insight: Plastic deformation is modeled as lattice-free oblate-spheroid transformation zones (OSTZs), from which dislocations emerge only as a collective limit rather than a primary assumption.
engineering_relevance: This offers a unified constitutive framework for plasticity across crystals, grain boundaries, glasses, ceramics, and nanocrystals where conventional lattice-based dislocation theory is incomplete.
method: Use Eshelby inclusion theory to define a shear-eigenstrained oblate spheroidal transformation zone.
Show that a single OSTZ gives a finite, non-singular elastic dipole field.
Demonstrate that co-planar chains of OSTZs reproduce Peierls–Nabarro dislocations and yield a host-lattice-set nucleation threshold.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports validation against grain-boundary-sliding data plus literature across metals, ceramics, and bulk metallic glasses, and a 41-system compilation with fitted dilatational and shear eigenstrains reproduced within 2% and 15%, respectively.
watch_item: The framework is strong analytically, but practical deployment will depend on how well OSTZ parameters and host-lattice critical lengths can be identified for new materials and loading paths.
paper_title: Oblate Spheroid Excitation Theory: A Unified, Lattice-Free Foundation for Plastic Deformation from Which Dislocations Emerge as Collective Excitations
